union or drawing-together between them. No Gaelic Leaguer
that ever I knew of cared to lend his support to foreign games in
preference to the native ones encouraged by.the G.A.A. Have
the members of the G.A.A. reciprocated to anything like the same
extent in aiding the ideals of the Gaelic League, and in running
clear of foreign songs, dances, music, and language ?

I would have imagined that urged by the desire for Irish
nationality the governing body of the G.A.A. would have done
everything in their power to make their games and organization
work in with the attempt to revive Irish nationhood. I should
have thought that they would long ago have insisted upon at least
half the teams from the counties of Cork, Kerry, Galway and Mayo
making Irish the official language of the hurling field, as it is the
language of their homes. I should not have been surprised if they !
had insisted on at least a dozen of the words of command being
given in Irish all over Ireland. It would be no burden on the clubs
and it would lend a great additional significance to the work of
the Association in the eyes of outsiders, and of all Irishmen both
at home and abroad. It would be a gain and a strength in every
way to have this additional mark of nationality stamped upon

our games.

A ‘go to see a hurling match because it is the national game who
".would never go to look at a cricket match. Vet very often the
.‘players never think of any other aspect of the question than that
of the match alone. The idea that they are really and powerfully
contributing to the revival of Irish nationality has, T believe, never
{occuned to thousands of them. If this is true, it is a real misfortune
for Ireland, and it robs the game of half its zest and signiﬁca'nce
for the unthinking players.

I feel certain, however, that, as Ireland becomes more Irish,
 the G.A.A. will aid in reviving Irish nationality all along the line.
~ and .become a great, a powerful, and an enlightened ally of the’
3 Gael.xc League, in its secondary work, in so far as it makes for
sobriety, clean homes, and the struggle against consumption ;
». some day, too, it will help it in its primary struggle for the’
revival of Ireland’s language, without which the Irish nation
jill remain like a half plucked fowl.

THE GAME OF BOWLING.
BY
p. ud Walmdin.

ANY of the readers of the Gaelic Annual are not, 'm sure,
very familiar with this manly and purely Irish game. At
the outset let me say it has nothing to do with the bowling-green
as known to the Sapanac 7 reomin. Itis played along the roads
in many parts of Ireland, and is as racy of the soil as hurling, a.nd
only equalled by it.as a health-giving and muscle producing exercise.
It, too, like hurling and all our national games portrays the genuine
Irish character—determination, yet delicacy of touch—swiftness,
~ yet accuracy of judgment, being the chief characteristics of the
bowlplayer.
The game of bowling at present seems little known outside
Munster, though there is reason to believe it was once universally
. practised throughout the land. Mid,, South and West Cork, the
 Kenmare district in Kerry, and West Clare seem to be its strong-
holds at present. It is also practised a little in Limerick, but T
have never heard of it being played in Waterford or Tipperary.
Last year I was much interested to hear of a game known as
“bullet ” throwing being played in certain parts of County Louth,
and, being curious to see the game, I paid a visit to'a locality
‘where I was promised an opportunity of witnessing it. On my
arrival here I was much surprised to find some young lads casting
_ bowls along the road. They had no idea of the game as known
‘ in Munster, but were simply throwing the “ bullets” from one
I to another. < Needless to say, for old times sake, I indulged in
; - the luxury of a few casts. ;.
[ It appears these bowls remained as a relic of the past in a
|
I
|

family of the old stock that resides in the neighbourhood, and the
younger blood laying hands on the “ bullets " enjoyed themselves
as above described. {
This is the only place outside Munster where I have seen
anything in the nature of a game of bowls. I have it though on
good authority that “ bullet ” throwing is at present pmctised‘ in‘
Kildare and the Midlands. Hence my reason, so far, for asserting

a

15

_ that bowl-playing was once generally practised throughout Ireland.

Cork, Kerry and Clare, being as I have said the strongholds
of the game, T shall try to give an'idea of it as practised in these
counties. In Cork and Kerry the game is identical. In Clare

it differs, both as to the weight of the bowl and style of casting.

In the former counties the bowl varies in weight between sixteen
and thirty-two ounces, twenty-six or twenty-eight ounce bowls
being the favourites with most players. Those used in Clare range
between four and eight pounds in weight. Needless to say the

- bowls in all cases are spherical. Before entering into a description
~ of the game I'd wish to point out the different styles of casting.
~ In Clare the bowls are cast under-arm, so too in Cork and Kerry,
- with this difference, that in these counties the arm is round when

casting. This difference in styles is due to the difference in weight

of the bowls,—the heavier bowls as used in Clare being such as not

to permit of the ‘ winding’ of the arm with comfort.

ach is supplied with his bowl. In this case each plays for himself.
Take a single handed match of three for example. They start
play from a certain place to a pre-arranged point about a mile or
two djstant,‘ and whoever reaches this mark first or in the least
number of casts wins the ‘‘ score.” Such preliminaries as *‘ spinning
‘the coin,” “ choice bowls,” etc., having been gone through the
score begins. He who wins chéice bowls leads off, and casts his
owl as far as he can, either by ““lofting " it through the air or

is preferable, while rolling along is more advantageous on smooth
ads. The first player has the privilege of commencing at what-

_In certain parts of Munster, especially Cork, regular inter-

To many unacquainted with the game of bowling it may at
first appear very dangerous, but beyond what is common to all
_games there is in reality no danger. Accidents occur in all games
and bowling is no exception. As a matter of fact we seldom indeed
tear of an accident during the progress of a bowling game. True,
it occasionally happens that an onlooker receives a stroke of a
bowl, but in such cases it must be admitted the victim is invariably
at fault either through carelessness or indifference. As a matter
of fact the greatest precautions are taken and the greatest vigilence
exercised to prevent accidents. The onlookers divide into two

MUSIC AT OUR GAMES.

ONE will deny the beneficial and harmonising influence of
music, and particularly good national music. It increases
immensely the enjoyment of any occasion and serves in a most
admirable way to supply that lull in excitement, stimulation
in endeavour and elevation of spirit which dignify enthusiasm
and enhance all pleasure. Weare sorry to say that in many respects
great Gaelic fixtures have been denied the attractiveness which
a well-chosen, if brief, programme of music could lend to them,
and we are further of opinion that where music—mostly of a
second-class and boisterous character—is provided it is utilised
in a manner which robs it of its pleasant and beneficent effects.
While in no way desirous of discouraging the custom of bands
accompanying local or county teams we would point out that the
- usual “ playing on to the field ” is a misuse of any music. Instead
of lending its elevating effects it becomes a blatant challenge,
tending to emphasise rather than diminish the baser passions.
- On the other hand, the performance of high-class compositions—
particularly of national and unifying inspiration—during intervals
prior to the contests would provide an admirable interlude, a most
‘appropriate accompaniment, and, to a large degree, sweep away
all that which athletic competition rouses, but which a true Gaelic
spirit laments—bitterness in defeat. or provocation in victory.
It would further servé to strip our best contests of that strange
grimness of enterprise so foreign to Gaelic feeling and of that idle
clamour which so often disgraces thoughtless partisans of popular
teams. If teams must be accompanied into the field by martial
strains they should proceed thither fogether, and the airs should
‘be significant of national rather than local or sectional associations.
‘When we consider how carefully the promoters of alien enter-
ainments provide that leaven of music which lightens their pro-
ceedings of whatever discription they may be, we cannot fail to
see how negligent our own people have been in this respect. Vet
we provide amusement for the masses of a traditionally musical
le, who are largely deprived of opportunity for hearing any-
hing but violent renderings of party tunes or discordant recitals
of foreign ** selections.”

A REVIEW OF GAELIC WORK IN CORK. -

HE Editor of the Gaelic Athletic Annual asked me to contribute
T something for the second edition of his splendid Record. I
was reluctant to consent, as I considered I had spoken and written
sufficiently about Gaelic matters lately, without again forcing
my views on the members of our Association. But on reflection
I decided to obey his request. I have selected above title so as
to justify the appearance of my name on the Table of Contents.
1 sincerely hope that your readers will find my brief attempt to
describe Gaelic Cork somewhat interesting and instructive ; if I
succeed in accomplishing this I am amply ~compensated for any
trouble I have gone to.

On the very establishment of G.A.A. the Southern Capital
was conspicuous both on the field and in the Legislative Chamber.
We had an extraordinary large number of affiliated clubs—far
exceeding any other County in Ireland. As far back as 1890
we annexed the All Ireland in both departments — stalwart
Midleton capturing the football, while famous Aghabullogue ac-
counted for the hurling “ Blue Riband.” In succeeding years we
also came out well, sometimes winning, sometimes losing, but always
keeping in front rank. Reference to Annual shows we have won
Munster Championship Hurling 10 and Football g times, also
All Treland Hurling 6 and Runners-up 4 times. ‘While in Football
we have secured premier honours once, and occupied second best
place no less than 8 times—the most unlucky record in the numerous
branches of Gaelic activity. But no despair: notwithstanding
terrible emigration or improved standard of play adopted by
other Counties. I rest assured, judging by present state of affairs,
that the Football Pendulum will swing Corkwards once again.
This statement probably seems unduly optimistic, but old Father
Time nevertheless will prove the accuracy or otherwise of this

%

cpinion.

in football—appeared to go from bad to worse. ‘This was saddening -

We triumphed in Senior match and drew in Junior; the latter

poor intelligence discovers in the attempt to keep Gaels from going
astray—*‘ Politics "—oh ! what an argument worthy of a brilliant
mind. No person who possesses the slightest idea of logic will
term such a proceeding thus. The garrison and our enemies
maintain it is because they refuse to see the wisdom and patriotism
of such rules. Of course, it matters not whether a man handles
a ball as in Rugby, dribbles as in Soccer, or catches as in Gaelic,
either form of play does not make him less Irish; but it is the
Anglicizing force, the driving tendency, the immense power which
these games possess for compelling us to focus our attention on the
ideals, sentiments, hopes, aspirations, aims and ambitions of our
hereditary oppressors. Our status as a nation ceases, we become
a shire of another country, we assimilate the habits of our masters
~ and forget the games which made our forefathers great and glorious
in those happy times—when Ireland was wholly Irish and when
Treland was wholly free. That this undesirable consummation
may be averted is, I believe, the prayer and wish of every Gael.
It should be more—it should be the work and constant work of all
 Gaels to prevent any flirting and coquetting with those who advocate -
~ Anglicization. We must fight that bugbear—that old man of the
sea must be sternly met and sternly dealt with. Nothing produces
such a great effect as determined action. If we are resolved
on our course of work, no whining of doubters, no head shaking
of wise acres will prevent our advance. Let the issue be straight—
Jour country or the garrison. Let every man choose, let us know
ho are our friends and who are our foes, we can then decide our
method of warfare. We can go boldly forth to defend our courntry’s
interests. ILet those who cannot make up their minds retire to
me peaceful abode, let the contest be sharp and vigorous, and
may God defend the right.

CORK CITY GAELIC FOOTBALL LEAGUE.

Annual Convention of Cork Football League was held in October,
1907. Convention decided (1) to start a Minor League, (2) estab-
lish Referees Association, (3) to organize a Munster College Com-
petition, (4) to advocate the formation of a National League
Championship, (5) to endeavour to erect a Pproper Dressing Pavilion
in Cork Park (League Grounds), (6) to start Leagues in County
Parliamentary Divisions. This programme was a pretty formid-
able one, but (1) has been carried through successfully ; (2) has
been performed, but not as completely as is desirable ; (3) organised
splendidly ; (4) not yet accomplished, but will be so in future ;
(5) with characteristic generosity, Sir John A. Arnott, Bart erected
a fine pavilion, which has proved to be a great advantage to Gaelic
Football in Cork. (6) Flourishing Hurling League has been es-
tablished by us in East Cork. Taking 1908 and comparing same
with 1907 we find Cork League has increased in strength—it had ouly
7 teams last year, now it boasts of 13 combinations. Next year
we hope to do even better. We are organising five other Leagues
in county, also Hurling and Football Leagues in N. Cork.
